Raffles Girls' Primary School

Primary 6 Science – Weighted Assessment 2 (2025) · Booklet A: 20 multiple-choice questions (40 marks) · Each question carries 2 marks in the paper; explanations are provided for every answer.

Booklet A — Multiple-Choice Questions (1–20)

Question 1

2 marks

Which of the following statements about photosynthesis in plants is true?

  1. Plants make glucose during photosynthesis.
  2. Plants trap heat energy from the Sun to make food.
  3. Excess food made in the plants are stored only in the fruits.
  4. Only the chlorophyll found in the stem of the plants are needed to make food.

Answer

Option 1. During photosynthesis a plant uses light energy to combine carbon dioxide and water into glucose (food). It traps light energy, not heat (so 2 is wrong); excess food can be stored in many parts such as roots, stem, leaves and seeds, not only the fruits (3 is wrong); and chlorophyll in any green part, especially the leaves, can make food (4 is wrong).

Question 2

2 marks

Larry conducted an experiment to find out whether carbon dioxide is needed for photosynthesis. He used the set-up as shown in the diagram.

Which of the following should Larry use as a control for his experiment?

Answer

Option 4. A fair control must be identical to the experimental set-up in every way except for the one variable being tested – carbon dioxide. Set-up (4) is the same plant under the same glass cover but with NO substance to remove carbon dioxide, so carbon dioxide is present. Option (1) changes the wrong variable (removes oxygen), and option (2) adds a black cloth which blocks light, introducing a second difference.

Question 3

2 marks

Jaya prepared two identical experimental set-ups, A and B, to find out if the light intensity would affect the rate at which water plants photosynthesise. A lamp was placed only beside set-up A for an hour as shown.

Which of the following identify the independent variable and dependent variable correctly?

Independent variableDependent variable
(1)The temperature of water in the beaker.The amount of water left in the beaker after one hour.
(2)The distance between the lamp and the set-up.The height of the gas column at the top of the test tube.
(3)The amount of water left in the beaker after one hour.The temperature of water in the beaker.
(4)The height of the gas column at the top of the test tube.The distance between the lamp and the set-up.

Answer

Option 2. The independent variable is what Jaya deliberately changes – here the light intensity, set by the distance between the lamp and the set-up. The dependent variable is what she measures to judge the rate of photosynthesis – the height of the gas (oxygen) column collected. Options (3) and (4) swap them around, and (1) names quantities that are not being controlled or measured here.

Question 4

2 marks

Thaddeus set up an experiment as shown in the diagram. After some time, the rate of photosynthesis of the plant decreased.

Which of the following best explains the decrease in the rate of photosynthesis?

  1. There was too much water in the set-up.
  2. There was insufficient oxygen in the set-up.
  3. There was insufficient nutrients in the water.
  4. There was insufficient carbon dioxide in the set-up.

Answer

Option 4. The plant is sealed inside an airtight glass cover, so the supply of carbon dioxide is limited. As photosynthesis uses up the carbon dioxide, less and less is available, so the rate falls. Oxygen is a product, not a requirement, of photosynthesis (2 is wrong), and neither excess water nor nutrient level is the limiting factor here.

Question 5

2 marks

Car tyres are tied together to form a barrier along go-kart racing tracks. This helps to protect the go-kart race drivers when accidents happen.

Which properties of the car tyres used to make the barrier help to ensure safety of the drivers?

  1. Strength and flexibility
  2. Magnetic and strength
  3. Magnetic and flexibility
  4. Strength and ability to float

Answer

Option 1. The tyres must be strong so the barrier does not break apart on impact, and flexible so they can squash and absorb the energy of the collision, cushioning the driver. Being magnetic or able to float has nothing to do with stopping a crashing go-kart safely.

Question 6

2 marks

Mun Wai dropped a 2 kg mass on material X from a fixed height as shown in the diagram.

He recorded the number of times the 2 kg mass was dropped before material X started to break, then repeated the experiment with materials Y and Z of identical thickness and length.

MaterialNumber of times the 2 kg mass was dropped before the material started to break
X36
Y48
Z72

Based on the results above, which of the following statements are true?

A  Material Y is the weakest material.
B  Material X is the strongest material.
C  Material Y is weaker than material Z.
D  Materials X and Y each has less strength than material Z.

  1. A and B only
  2. A and C only
  3. B and D only
  4. C and D only

Answer

Option 4. The more drops a material withstands, the stronger it is. Z (72) is the strongest and X (36) the weakest. So Y is weaker than Z (C is true) and both X and Y need fewer drops than Z, meaning each is less strong than Z (D is true). Statement A is wrong (X, not Y, is weakest) and B is wrong (Z, not X, is strongest).

Question 7

2 marks

Jason lowered a bar magnet into a tray of steel pins as shown in the diagram. He counted the number of steel pins that were attracted to each of the parts labelled A, B and C.

Which one of the following most likely shows the number of steel pins attracted to the bar magnet?

Part APart BPart C
(1)51012
(2)1022
(3)15312
(4)12153

Answer

Option 3. A bar magnet is strongest at its two poles (the ends, A and C) and weakest in the middle (B), so the ends should attract many pins and the centre very few. Only option (3) shows this pattern – many pins at A (15) and C (12) but few at B (3). The other options put too many pins at the middle.

Question 8

2 marks

The diagrams show the readings on a measuring scale before and after Seema placed a bar magnet 2 cm above bar H.

What can Seema conclude about bar H?

  1. Bar H is a magnet.
  2. Bar H is a steel rod.
  3. Bar H is a copper rod.
  4. Bar H is a wooden rod.

Answer

Option 1. The reading increased from 10 g to 13 g, so bar H was pushed downwards harder onto the scale. A downward push means the magnet above is repelling bar H. Repulsion can only happen between two magnets with like poles facing each other, so bar H must itself be a magnet. A steel rod would be attracted upwards (the reading would have decreased), and copper and wood are non-magnetic and would not change the reading at all.

Question 9

2 marks

The diagram shows a compass which has a small magnet that can rotate freely. The arrow points to the north pole of the magnet.

Four bar magnets were arranged such that they were attracted to one another. A compass was then placed near end K and the direction of the compass needle is as shown.

What would be the direction of the needle when the compass is placed at P?

Answer

Option 2. The four magnets attract one another, so their poles alternate around the chain. End K behaves as one pole (shown by the compass next to it), and end P is the opposite end of the chain and therefore the opposite pole. The compass at P must point in the direction shown in option (2), the mirror image of the needle seen at K.

Question 10

2 marks

The diagrams show a bird during three stages, A (taking off), B (flying) and C (landing) of flight.

At which stage(s) do/does gravity act on the bird?

  1. B only
  2. A and B only
  3. A and C only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 4. Gravity (the pull of the Earth) acts on every object that has mass, all the time, no matter what it is doing. The bird has mass during taking off, flying and landing, so gravity acts on it at all three stages – A, B and C.

Question 11

2 marks

A block of ice was pushed in the direction indicated by the arrows. The block moved along the ground to position S and then to position T, where it stopped.

Which of the following shows the forces acting on the block of ice while it was being pushed to position S and when it stopped at T? (✓ = present)

Being pushed to S – FrictionWeightStopped at T – FrictionWeight
(1)✓✓✓
(2)✓✓✓
(3)✓✓✓
(4)✓✓✓

Answer

Option 3. Weight (the force of gravity) always acts on the block, both while moving and at rest, so it must be present in both columns. Friction acts to oppose motion: while the block is sliding to S there is friction between the ice and the ground, but once it has stopped at T it is no longer moving, so there is no friction acting. Only option (3) shows friction and weight while moving, and weight only at rest.

Question 12

2 marks

A box was released from the top of a ramp with surface W, and the time taken to reach the bottom was recorded. The experiment was repeated with three identical ramps having surfaces X, Y and Z.

Type of surfaceTime taken to reach the bottom of ramp (s)
W12
X16
Y4
Z8

Which of the following shows the surfaces of the ramps with the least to most amount of friction?

Least → Most
(1)WXYZ
(2)XWZY
(3)YZWX
(4)ZXYW

Answer

Option 3. More friction slows the box down, so a longer time means more friction. Ranking the times from shortest to longest gives Y (4 s), Z (8 s), W (12 s), X (16 s) – which is the order from least to most friction. This matches option (3).

Question 13

2 marks

Two different springs, J and K, have the same length. The cubes are stacked: one cube rests on spring J, and that spring (together with a second cube) rests on spring K, which stands on the table top. Each spring is compressed by 5 cm.

Based on the information above, which of the following correctly shows the relationship between the elastic spring force and compression for springs J and K?

Answer

Option 4. Because the cubes are stacked, spring J only carries the single top cube, while spring K below carries that cube and the second cube above it – roughly double the load. Yet both springs compress by the same 5 cm. To be squashed the same amount under a much larger weight, spring K must push back with a larger elastic spring force for the same compression, so K is the stiffer (harder) spring and J is the softer one. On the compression-against-force graph this means that for a given force J compresses more than K, so J's line is steeper and K's line is shallower, both rising straight from the origin. Option (4) is the only graph that shows this – J steeper than K. Options (1) and (2) show the lines falling, and (3) wrongly makes K the steeper (softer-looking) line.

Question 14

2 marks

Elena walked barefoot on some ceramic tiles and then on the wooden flooring in a room. The room temperature was 28 °C.

Which of the following statements most likely explains why her feet felt cold on the ceramic tiles but not on the wooden flooring?

  1. The feet gained heat from the ceramic tiles.
  2. The ceramic tiles is a poorer conductor of heat than the wooden flooring.
  3. The wooden flooring is a poorer conductor of heat than the ceramic tiles.
  4. The temperature of the wooden flooring is higher than the temperature of the ceramic tiles.

Answer

Option 3. Both surfaces are at the same room temperature (28 °C), so the cold feeling is not about temperature (4 is wrong). The feet are warmer than the floor, so heat flows from the feet to the floor (1 is wrong). Ceramic is a good conductor and draws heat away from the feet quickly, making them feel cold, while wood is a poorer conductor and removes heat slowly, so the feet do not feel cold. Therefore the wooden flooring is the poorer conductor.

Question 15

2 marks

Keagen poured 100 ml and 700 ml of boiling water into identical beakers, X and Y, respectively. (Beaker X has the smaller amount of water.)

Keagen measured the temperature of the water every minute and recorded it in a graph. Which of the following graphs show the temperature change in the water in containers X and Y?

Answer

Option 3. Both samples start at the same boiling temperature (100 °C) and cool down. The smaller amount of water (X, 100 ml) has less heat to lose and a larger surface-area-to-volume exposure, so it cools faster and its curve drops below Y. The larger amount (Y, 700 ml) stays warmer for longer, so its curve lies above X. Option (3) correctly shows X cooling more quickly (lower curve) than Y.

Question 16

2 marks

Two empty identical flasks, J and K, were connected with a glass tube containing an ink drop. Flask J was wrapped with a cold cloth and flask K with a warm cloth, as shown.

Which of the following correctly explains the movement of the ink drop after five minutes?

Movement of ink dropExplanation
(1)Moved towards flask JAir in flask J gained heat from the cold cloth and contracted
(2)Moved towards flask JAir in flask K gained heat from the warm cloth and expanded
(3)Moved towards flask KAir in flask K lost heat to the warm cloth and expanded
(4)Moved towards flask KAir in flask J lost heat to the cold cloth and contracted

Answer

Option 2. The warm cloth on flask K gives heat to the air inside it, so that air gains heat and expands. The expanding air pushes the ink drop away from flask K – that is, towards flask J. (The air in flask J, against the cold cloth, loses heat and contracts, which also draws the drop towards J.) Option (2) gives the correct direction with the correct reason: air in K gained heat and expanded.

Question 17

2 marks

Which of the following is/are renewable source(s) of energy?

A  Wind
B  Sunlight
C  Tidal waves
D  Natural gas

  1. B only
  2. D only
  3. A and C only
  4. A, B and C only

Answer

Option 4. Renewable energy sources are naturally replenished and will not run out – wind, sunlight and tidal waves all fit this description. Natural gas is a fossil fuel that takes millions of years to form and will eventually be used up, so it is non-renewable. Hence A, B and C only.

Question 18

2 marks

The diagram shows a sky lantern made of paper. When the candle is lit, the sky lantern flies into the sky.

Which one of the following correctly shows the energy conversion when the candle is lit and the sky lantern rises into the sky?

  1. Chemical potential energy → light + heat energy → kinetic energy
  2. Kinetic energy → gravitational potential energy → heat + light energy
  3. Light energy → heat energy → kinetic energy → gravitational potential energy
  4. Chemical potential energy → light + heat energy → kinetic + gravitational potential energy

Answer

Option 4. The candle wax stores chemical potential energy. Burning converts it to light and heat energy. The heat warms the air, which rises and lifts the lantern, giving it kinetic energy as it moves and gravitational potential energy as it gains height. Option (4) is the only one that includes both the kinetic and the gravitational potential energy the rising lantern gains, starting correctly from chemical potential energy.

Question 19

2 marks

Gemma aimed and shot a basketball into the hoop. The arrows indicate the direction in which the ball bounced before coming to a stop at P. (The ball travels K → L near the hoop, then bounces down to M, up to N, down to O and stops at P.)

Which of the following statement(s) is/are true?

A  The ball has no kinetic energy at O.
B  The ball has no potential energy at K.
C  The ball has the most potential energy at L.
D  All the energy has been destroyed when the ball came to a stop at P.

  1. C only
  2. A and B only
  3. A and C only
  4. B and D only

Answer

Option 1. L is the highest point of the ball's path (at the hoop), so the ball has the most gravitational potential energy there – statement C is true. Statement A is false: the ball is still moving at O, so it has kinetic energy. Statement B is false: K is above the ground, so the ball still has potential energy there. Statement D is false: energy is never destroyed – at P it has been converted to heat and sound, not lost. So only C is correct.

Question 20

2 marks

A ball was released from rest on a smooth ramp as shown. The ball rolled from point A (top of one side) down through B (bottom of the curve) and up towards C and D.

Which graph shows the changes in the amount of kinetic energy of the ball as it rolled from point A to C?

Answer

Option 2. The ball starts from rest at A, so its kinetic energy at A is zero. As it rolls down to the lowest point B it speeds up, gaining the most kinetic energy at B. Climbing back up towards C it slows down, so its kinetic energy decreases again but is still more than zero (C is lower than A). Option (2) shows zero at A, the tallest bar at B and a medium bar at C – the correct pattern.
